codeReview.summary: Solid, well-structured refactor from single-framebuffer to multi-surface compositing. The drawing library gains CompositeSurface struct and composite_surfaces() with insertion sort by z-order, negative-offset clipping, and blit_blend delegation for right/bottom clipping. The compositor allocates 6 independent surface buffers (background, content, title shadow, status shadow, title chrome, status chrome), renders each independently, and composites back-to-front into the framebuffer each frame. 9 new tests comprehensively cover z-ordering, alpha blending, visibility filtering, clipping (both negative and right/bottom edge), 3-layer stacking, and empty list handling. The insertion sort uses strict '>' comparison making it stable for equal z values (important since title bar and status bar share z=20). Visual verification confirms correct boot frame and text editing.

issues.description: MAX_SURFACES ceiling of 16 silently truncates the surfaces array without any diagnostic. If more than 16 surfaces are passed, the excess are silently dropped. Consider adding a debug assertion or comment explaining the cap. Currently the compositor uses 6 so this is not a problem, but could cause silent bugs if surface count grows.

Damage tracking is effectively disabled — every frame does a full-screen GPU transfer (rect_count: 0). The code comment acknowledges this ('full-screen transfer for now'), and the rationale is sound (multi-surface compositing touches most pixels each frame). However, this is a performance regression from the previous damage-tracked partial transfers. For future features with per-surface dirty tracking, this will need to be revisited.

CompositeSurface construction is duplicated between the initial frame render (~line 601) and the event loop (~line 740). Both blocks build identical surface references from the same buffers. This is acceptable in bare-metal no_std Rust where abstractions are expensive, but the duplication is notable — any surface layout change must be made in two places.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The architecture.md file (.factory/library/architecture.md) was NOT updated to reflect the multi-surface compositing model. It still describes Drawing Library primitives as 'fill_rect, fill_rect_blend, draw_line, blit, blit_blend, draw_coverage' without mentioning CompositeSurface or composite_surfaces(). The last line says 'No rounded rects, no blur, no gradients yet' but the codebase now includes fill_gradient_v (for drop shadows) and composite_surfaces. The Display Pipeline section also doesn't describe the surface layer model (z-ordering, surface allocation, per-surface rendering).
sharedStateObservations.evidence: .factory/library/architecture.md bottom line: 'Primitives: fill_rect, fill_rect_blend, draw_line, blit, blit_blend, draw_coverage' and 'No rounded rects, no blur, no gradients yet' — both outdated. The file IS in modified state in git (git status shows 'M .factory/library/architecture.md') but the changes were NOT included in commit c0449a2.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The AGENTS.md coding conventions don't mention the multi-surface compositing pattern or the new surface allocation approach (alloc_surface_buf + make_surf + render_*_surface functions). Future workers modifying the compositor or adding new surfaces would benefit from knowing the convention: each surface has a dedicated render function, surfaces are allocated once at startup and re-rendered in-place each frame.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: system/services/compositor/main.rs: render_background(), render_content_surface(), render_title_bar(), render_status_bar(), render_title_shadow(), render_status_shadow() — all follow the pattern of taking &mut Surface and rendering one layer. This pattern is new and not documented.

summary: Feature passes review. The multi-surface compositor refactor is well-implemented: CompositeSurface struct with z-ordered back-to-front compositing via blit_blend, 6 independently-rendered surfaces, stable insertion sort for z-ordering, proper edge clipping. 9 new tests cover the key behaviors. The only notable trade-off is disabling damage-tracked partial transfers in favor of full-screen compositing each frame, which is reasonable given the compositing model touches most pixels. Architecture.md needs updating to reflect the new multi-surface model and gradient primitives.
